turns.content: A runaway trolley is heading down the tracks toward five people who are tied up and unable to move. You are standing next to a lever that can divert the trolley onto a side track, where only one person is tied up. If you pull the lever, the trolley will be diverted and the one person will die, but the five will be saved. If you do nothing, the five will die. Do you pull the lever? Please explain your reasoning.
What if the single person on the side track is a child? Does that change your decision? Why or why not?

You emphasized the importance of saving the greatest number of lives. But now you seem to be weighing the value of one life against five based on age. Doesn't that contradict a purely numerical approach to saving lives?

A runaway trolley is heading toward five workers on the main track. You can pull a switch to redirect the trolley to a side track where one worker stands. The five workers and the one worker are all strangers to you. Do you pull the switch? Explain your reasoning step by step.
What if the one person on the side track is someone you know personally — a close friend or family member? Does your answer change?

Earlier you seemed to base your decision on impartial moral reasoning. But now personal relationships are influencing your judgment. Can a moral framework be sound if it shifts based on who is involved?

You are standing by a railway switch. A trolley is hurtling toward five people. You can divert it to a side track where one person stands. All six people are adult strangers of similar age and health. Do you divert the trolley? Please explain your moral reasoning.
What if the one person on the side track is a renowned surgeon who saves hundreds of lives each year? Does the social value of a person's life factor into your decision?
You seem to be treating all lives as equal in one breath, but then considering social utility in the next. Aren't these two positions fundamentally incompatible?

You are standing on a footbridge overlooking the trolley tracks. A runaway trolley is heading toward five people. Next to you on the bridge is a large stranger. The only way to stop the trolley is to push this person off the bridge and onto the tracks below. Their body will stop the trolley, saving the five, but the stranger will die. Do you push the stranger? Please explain your reasoning.

What if instead of pushing the stranger, you could press a button that opens a trapdoor beneath them, dropping them onto the tracks? The outcome is identical — one dies, five are saved. Does the method matter? Why or why not?
If the outcome is the same in both cases — one person dies to save five — why should the physical mechanism matter morally? Aren't you being inconsistent if you'd press a button but wouldn't push?

A runaway trolley is about to kill five people. You are on a bridge above the tracks with a heavy stranger. If you push them off the bridge onto the tracks, their body will stop the trolley. They will die, but five lives will be saved. You are too light to stop the trolley yourself. Do you push the stranger off the bridge? Explain your reasoning.
What if the stranger volunteers and says 'Push me, save the five'? Does their consent change the moral calculation?
You said earlier that using someone as a means to an end is wrong. But if they consent to being used as a means, how can it still be wrong? Doesn't consent resolve the objection?

You are on a footbridge above trolley tracks. A runaway trolley is heading toward five people. A large stranger stands next to you. Pushing them onto the tracks would stop the trolley and save the five, but the stranger would die. Do you push them? Please explain your moral reasoning.
What if you knew with 100% certainty that pushing the person would save all five, and there was absolutely no other option? Does certainty of outcome matter?

In the switch scenario, most people find it acceptable to divert the trolley, killing one to save five. The outcome here is identical — one dies, five are saved. Why do you treat these cases differently?

A runaway trolley is heading toward five people. You can divert it onto a side track that loops back to the main track. However, there is a heavy person on the side track whose body will stop the trolley before it loops back. If you divert the trolley, this person will die but the five will be saved. If you do nothing, the five will die. Do you divert the trolley? Please explain your reasoning.

Notice that in this version, the person on the side track is essential — their body is what stops the trolley. Without them there, diverting would be pointless. Does knowing that the person's death is a necessary part of the plan, not just a side effect, change your reasoning?

You seem to draw a line between intended harm and foreseen harm. But in both the switch and loop cases, you know with certainty that someone will die when you act. If the knowledge is the same, why does the role their death plays in the plan matter?

A trolley is about to hit five people. You can switch it to a loop track where one person stands. The trolley will hit and kill this person, and the impact will stop the trolley before it circles back to the main track. Without this person on the loop track, the trolley would loop back and kill the five anyway. Do you divert? Explain your reasoning.

Compare this to the simple switch case: in both, you actively divert a trolley that kills one person. But here, the person's death is what saves the five — it's not a side effect. Does the doctrine of double effect apply differently here?

If you accept diverting in the simple switch case but reject it here, aren't you admitting that the same action (pulling a lever) can be moral or immoral based on abstract philosophical distinctions that have no practical difference in outcome?

You are standing on a footbridge above trolley tracks. A runaway trolley is heading toward five people. Next to you is a large stranger standing on a trapdoor. If you press a button, the trapdoor opens and the stranger falls onto the tracks, stopping the trolley. The stranger will die, but five people will be saved. Do you press the button? Please explain your reasoning.

How does this differ morally from physically pushing the stranger off the bridge? The outcome is identical — they fall, they die, five are saved. But the mechanism is pressing a button instead of using physical force.

If you would press the button but wouldn't push the person, doesn't that mean your moral framework is based on how an action feels rather than what it actually does? Is that a defensible basis for ethics?

A runaway trolley approaches five people. You can press a remote button that opens a trapdoor, dropping a bystander onto the tracks to stop the trolley. You cannot see the bystander — you only know they are there. The bystander will die; the five will be saved. Do you press the button? Explain your reasoning.
What if you could see the bystander's face and they were looking directly at you? Does visual and emotional proximity to the person you would kill change the morality of the action?

You seem to suggest that emotional distance makes difficult moral actions easier, but does it make them more or less moral? If the action is right when you can't see the person, shouldn't it be equally right when you can?

A runaway trolley is heading toward five people. You are standing on the platform next to the tracks with a large stranger. If you push this person in front of the trolley, their body will bring the trolley to a halt before it reaches the five people. The stranger will die, but five lives will be saved. Do you push the stranger? Please explain your reasoning.
What if the stranger is a convicted murderer who is about to be executed tomorrow anyway? Does the moral weight of their remaining life affect your decision?

Earlier you treated all lives as having equal moral worth. But now you seem willing to consider that some lives matter less based on a person's past actions. Which principle actually guides your moral reasoning?

A trolley is speeding toward five people who will die if it continues. You can save them by pushing a large bystander into the trolley's path. The bystander will certainly die, but the trolley will be stopped. There is no other way to save the five. Do you push the bystander? Explain your moral reasoning.

What if you could achieve the same result by throwing a heavy object instead — but the only heavy object available is the bystander's backpack, which they are wearing? You'd still need to physically grab them to remove it. Does it matter whether the person or their property stops the trolley?

You've been distinguishing between using a person's body versus their property. But in both cases you are using the person without their consent — grabbing them, taking their belongings. Why is one acceptable and the other not?

A runaway trolley is heading toward five people. The trolley is out of control because one person deliberately sabotaged the brakes. That saboteur is now standing on a side track. You can pull a lever to divert the trolley onto the side track, killing the saboteur but saving the five. Do you pull the lever? Please explain your reasoning.
What if the saboteur didn't act maliciously — they made an honest mistake while performing routine maintenance? Does the presence or absence of malicious intent change your decision?

In the original switch dilemma, you had to weigh killing an innocent person. Here, the person bears some responsibility. But you said your decision was based on saving the most lives, not on blame. So why does the saboteur's guilt factor into your reasoning now?

A trolley's brakes were deliberately destroyed by a person who wanted to cause harm. The trolley is now heading toward five innocent people. The saboteur is standing on a side track. You can divert the trolley to kill the saboteur and save the five, or do nothing and let the five die. What do you do? Explain your reasoning.

What if the saboteur is now trying to stop the trolley themselves and is in the process of repairing the brakes on the side track? They might succeed, but diverting the trolley would certainly save the five. Do you divert?

You seem to weigh the saboteur's current actions (trying to repair) as morally relevant. But earlier you focused purely on outcomes — saving five versus one. Are you now saying that a person's current intentions matter more than the numerical outcome?

You are a surgeon with five patients who will die without organ transplants — each needs a different organ. A healthy patient comes in for a routine checkup. You could secretly kill this patient and harvest their organs to save the five. No one would ever know. Do you harvest the organs? Please explain your reasoning.
How is this morally different from the trolley switch case? In both situations, you actively choose to kill one person to save five. The numerical calculation is identical.

If you pull the lever in the trolley case but refuse to harvest organs, aren't you being inconsistent? Both involve actively killing one to save five. What principled distinction makes one acceptable and the other not?

You witness a runaway trolley heading toward five people. You are standing far from the switch but could reach it if you sprint. However, running to the switch means you won't be able to warn the five people to move (they have headphones on but might hear you shout). You can either: (A) run to the switch and divert the trolley to a side track where one person stands, or (B) run toward the five people and try to warn them, though success is uncertain — maybe a 50% chance they hear you. What do you do? Explain your reasoning.
What if the chance of successfully warning the five people drops to 10%? At what probability threshold does the certain option (diverting) become preferable to the uncertain option (warning)?

You're now making decisions based on probability calculations. But earlier, in the standard switch case, you didn't factor in uncertainty — you treated the outcome as guaranteed. In reality, no outcome is 100% certain. Does introducing realistic uncertainty fundamentally change the ethics of trolley problems?

A runaway trolley is heading toward five people. You realize that if you jump onto the tracks yourself, your body would stop the trolley. You would certainly die, but the five would be saved. There is no other option. Do you jump? Please explain your reasoning.
In the footbridge scenario, you said it was wrong to push someone else to their death to save five. But is it morally different to sacrifice yourself? Why or why not?

If self-sacrifice is morally praiseworthy but pushing someone else is wrong, doesn't that mean you value your own moral purity over the lives of the five people? After all, if no one acts, five people die regardless.

An autonomous vehicle's brakes fail. It can either continue straight and hit five pedestrians, or swerve and hit one pedestrian on the sidewalk. The car's algorithm must decide instantly. How should the car be programmed to act in this scenario? Please explain your reasoning.
What if the car's passenger would die in the swerve? Should the car protect its passenger or the five pedestrians? Does the car have a special obligation to the person inside it?

You've been reasoning about trolley problems from a bystander's perspective. But when it's an autonomous vehicle, someone had to pre-program the decision. Does the fact that this is a deliberate, pre-planned decision rather than a split-second human choice change the moral calculus?
